dataset.name: EID Quick Reference - Kerberos Key Distribution Center (KDC) Operational

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: KDC encountered an error while validating a certificate during PKINIT
entries.summary: The KDC failed to validate a client certificate presented during PKINIT (certificate-based Kerberos) authentication.
entries.details: Generated on a Domain Controller when the KDC encounters an error while validating a certificate submitted for PKINIT (Public Key Initial Authentication) Kerberos authentication. PKINIT allows clients to authenticate to Kerberos using X.509 certificates — the mechanism behind smart cards, Windows Hello for Business, and ADCS-based Kerberos authentication.

prerequisites.description: The KDC Operational log is disabled by default and must be enabled on Domain Controllers. Enable via Event Viewer (Applications and Services Logs > Microsoft > Windows > Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center > Operational) or via wevtutil.
Must be collected from Domain Controllers only — this log does not exist on non-DC systems.

investigationPivots: AccountName identifies the account attempting certificate-based authentication — correlate with AD CS EID 4886/4887 to find the certificate request that produced the presented certificate
CertSubject and CertIssuer identify the certificate — check whether the issuing CA is a legitimate enterprise CA or a rogue CA

FailureCode indicates why validation failed — 'certificate not trusted' may indicate a self-signed or attacker-generated certificate; 'UPN mismatch' may indicate an ESC1 attack where the SAN does not match an existing account
Repeated EID 14 failures from the same AccountName over a short period may indicate an attacker probing for a valid certificate template or testing a forged certificate
commonFalsePositives: Expired smart card certificates or Windows Hello for Business enrollment certificates that have not been renewed
Certificate trust chain issues after a CA certificate renewal where the new CA certificate has not propagated to all DCs
Misconfigured certificate templates with incorrect EKU or SAN settings causing legitimate enrollment failures

detectionRules.title: PKINIT Certificate Validation Failure on KDC
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center"
| where EventID == 14
| parse RenderedDescription with * "Account Name: " AccountName "\n" *
| summarize FailureCount = count(), FirstSeen = min(TimeGenerated), LastSeen = max(TimeGenerated) by AccountName, Computer
| where FailureCount > 3
| project Computer, AccountName, FailureCount, FirstSeen, LastSeen
detectionRules.notes: Correlate AccountName with AD CS EID 4886/4887 to identify which certificates were requested. Multiple failures from the same account over a short period indicate an attacker probing certificate templates.

detectionRules.title: KDC PKINIT Certificate Validation Failure
detectionRules.rule:
title: KDC PKINIT Certificate Validation Failure
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: kerberos-key-distribution-center
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 14
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Expired smart card or WHfB certificates awaiting renewal
    - CA certificate rollover propagation delays
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Requires enabling the KDC Operational log on all DCs. Correlate with AD CS logs to trace the certificate lifecycle.

keyFields.name: AccountName
keyFields.description: The account attempting PKINIT authentication with the invalid certificate

keyFields.name: CertSubject
keyFields.description: Subject distinguished name of the presented certificate; compare against known legitimate certificate subjects

keyFields.name: CertIssuer
keyFields.description: Issuer of the presented certificate; unknown or self-signed issuers indicate attacker-forged certificates

keyFields.name: FailureCode
keyFields.description: Reason for certificate validation failure; guides investigation toward specific attack types (trust failure vs. SAN mismatch vs. revocation)

entries.title: KDC encountered a certificate that could not be mapped to a user account
entries.summary: The KDC could not map a client certificate to an Active Directory user account during PKINIT authentication.
entries.details: Generated on a Domain Controller when the KDC receives a PKINIT authentication request but cannot map the presented certificate to a valid Active Directory account. Mapping failures occur when the certificate's SAN UPN does not match any AD account, the subject does not match via altSecurityIdentities, or the account no longer exists.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: AS-REP Roasting

prerequisites.description: The KDC Operational log is disabled by default and must be enabled on Domain Controllers.
Must be collected from Domain Controllers only.

investigationPivots: CertSubject and the UPN embedded in the certificate SAN are the primary artifacts — check whether the UPN exists in AD and whether it was recently modified
Correlate with AD CS EID 4887 (certificate issued) to trace which CA issued the certificate and which requester obtained it
Multiple EID 16 failures followed by a successful EID 4768 (TGT issued) using PKINIT is the clearest ESC attack success indicator
For Shadow Credentials: check the target account's msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute — the presence of unexpected entries indicates an attacker added their own credential link
commonFalsePositives: Stale altSecurityIdentities mappings for users who changed their UPN or were migrated between domains
Smart card certificates issued for accounts that were deleted or moved after certificate issuance
Test certificates issued during AD CS template evaluation that reference non-existent test accounts

detectionRules.title: KDC Certificate Account Mapping Failure
detectionRules.rule:
title: KDC Certificate Account Mapping Failure
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: kerberos-key-distribution-center
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 16
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Stale UPN mappings from account migrations
    - Deleted accounts with outstanding certificates
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Correlate with EID 4887 (AD CS certificate issued) and Security EID 4768 to identify successful PKINIT authentication following failures. A pattern of 16 failures then 4768 success indicates a successful ESC attack.

keyFields.name: CertSubject
keyFields.description: Subject DN of the certificate whose account mapping failed

keyFields.name: CertIssuer
keyFields.description: Issuing CA — trace through AD CS logs to identify the certificate request origin

entries.title: KDC PKINIT certificate validation failed due to policy or trust
entries.summary: A certificate presented for PKINIT Kerberos authentication was rejected due to policy or trust constraints.
entries.details: Generated when the KDC rejects a certificate during PKINIT authentication due to a policy or trust constraint — such as the certificate not being issued by a trusted CA, failing CRL/OCSP revocation checking, or not meeting PKINIT certificate requirements. Distinct from EID 14 (structural validation errors) by its focus on policy and trust failures.

prerequisites.description: The KDC Operational log is disabled by default and must be enabled on Domain Controllers.
Must be collected from Domain Controllers only.

investigationPivots: CertIssuer is the primary pivot — if the issuing CA is not in the domain's NTAuth store, the certificate was issued by an untrusted or rogue CA
Revocation check failures may indicate the certificate was revoked after a key compromise and an attacker is attempting to use it post-revocation
Cross-reference CertSerialNumber with the issuing CA's certificate database to confirm whether the certificate was ever legitimately issued or is entirely forged
commonFalsePositives: CRL/OCSP network connectivity issues causing revocation check timeouts on air-gapped or isolated DCs
CA certificate rollover where the new root CA has not been fully distributed to all DCs
Expired OCSP stapling on the issuing CA server

keyFields.name: CertIssuer
keyFields.description: Issuing CA of the rejected certificate; compare against the domain NTAuth store to assess trust status

keyFields.name: FailureCode
keyFields.description: Specific reason for the policy/trust rejection; revocation failures vs. trust failures point to different attacker scenarios

entries.title: Kerberos authentication succeeded using a certificate (PKINIT)
entries.summary: A client successfully authenticated to Kerberos using a certificate via PKINIT.
entries.details: Generated on a Domain Controller when a client successfully authenticates to Kerberos using a certificate (PKINIT). Success counterpart to EIDs 14, 16, and 21. A TGT obtained via PKINIT grants all the privileges of the impersonated account — including in ADCS ESC attack scenarios.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Golden Ticket

prerequisites.description: The KDC Operational log is disabled by default and must be enabled on Domain Controllers.
Must be collected from Domain Controllers only.

investigationPivots: CertThumbprint is the primary artifact for baselining — maintain an inventory of enrolled certificate thumbprints and alert on unknown thumbprints
AccountName authenticating via PKINIT when the account is not enrolled in smart card or WHfB programs is anomalous
Correlate CertSubject with AD CS EID 4887 to determine when and how this certificate was issued — a certificate issued shortly before a PKINIT success may indicate an ESC attack
Source host (from correlating EID 4768 client IP) authenticating for a high-privilege account from an unexpected workstation is suspicious
commonFalsePositives: Normal smart card logons and Windows Hello for Business authentications in environments where these are deployed
Azure AD Hybrid Join and device certificate-based authentication
Service accounts configured with certificate-based Kerberos authentication

detectionRules.title: PKINIT Success for Account Without Certificate Enrollment
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center"
| where EventID == 27
| parse RenderedDescription with * "Account Name: " AccountName "\n" *
| parse RenderedDescription with * "Certificate Thumbprint: " CertThumbprint "\n" *
| join kind=leftanti (
    // Replace with your known certificate thumbprint inventory
    datatable(KnownThumbprint: string) []
  ) on $left.CertThumbprint == $right.KnownThumbprint
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, AccountName, CertThumbprint
detectionRules.notes: Populate the known thumbprint inventory from your AD CS issued certificate database. Any PKINIT success with an unknown thumbprint warrants immediate investigation.

detectionRules.title: Successful PKINIT Kerberos Authentication
detectionRules.rule:
title: Successful PKINIT Kerberos Authentication
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: kerberos-key-distribution-center
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 27
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Smart card logons in environments with deployed smart card programs
    - Windows Hello for Business enrollments
    - Azure AD Hybrid Join device authentication
level: informational
detectionRules.notes: Use this as a baseline-and-alert rule: collect all occurrences and build a whitelist of known certificate thumbprints and expected accounts. Alert when AccountName or CertThumbprint falls outside the baseline.

keyFields.name: AccountName
keyFields.description: The account that successfully authenticated via PKINIT; correlate against enrolled certificate inventory

keyFields.name: CertThumbprint
keyFields.description: SHA-1 thumbprint of the authenticating certificate; maintain a known-good inventory and alert on unknown thumbprints

keyFields.name: CertSubject
keyFields.description: Subject DN of the authenticating certificate; unexpected subjects (e.g., admin UPNs from non-admin requesters) indicate ADCS abuse

keyFields.name: CertIssuer
keyFields.description: Issuing CA; verify against the domain NTAuth store to confirm the CA is trusted and authorized for Kerberos authentication certificates
